title: Fish and chips
subtitle: Beer-battered cod, twice-cooked chips.
excerpt: Fillets of cod or haddock dipped in a cold beer batter and fried golden, alongside twice-cooked chips with crisp shells and fluffy middles. Pub-style at home. Serves four.
sourceNotes: The twice-cooked chip method appears in Auguste Escoffier, A Guide to Modern Cookery (1903), available via public-domain reprint. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), describes coating fillets in light flour batter before frying; the beer in the batter is a twentieth-century English chip-shop refinement.

recipe.freezeNotes: Neither the batter nor the chips survive freezing well; both lose their crispness.
recipe.batchNotes: Frying in domestic pans tops out at four portions; do more in waves and keep the cooked fish warm in a low oven.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The first chip-fry can be done up to two hours ahead and held at room temperature; the second fry happens just before serving.

recipeTools.notes: A wide deep saucepan with high sides works equally well.
With a sugar or instant-read thermometer for oil temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A first pass through 140°C oil cooks the inside of the chip without browning it. The chip is then drained and held; the second pass at 190°C crisps the shell. Two-stage frying gives the fluffy-middle, crisp-shell texture.

content.text: Two thick fillets of cod or haddock per pair, peeled potatoes cut into thumb-thick chips, a bowl of cold beer batter mixed at the last moment. Chips fried twice — once gently to cook the inside, once hard to crisp the shell. Fish fried once in a hot bath until the batter is bubbled gold and the flesh inside flakes cleanly.

The trick is two-stage frying. The blanching pass cooks the chip through at 140°C without browning. The crisp pass at 190°C, after the chips have cooled, gives the chip-shop shatter. The batter wants to be cold, lumpy, and freshly mixed; resting it kills the carbonation that gives the puffed shell.

attrs.title: Oil safety
attrs.body: Use a wide, heavy pan with high sides. Fill no more than a third full. Keep a metal lid within reach to smother a flare-up. Never leave hot oil unattended on the hob. Run cold water over a burn and seek medical care if needed.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: thick fillet, 180-200 g each, skinless, pin-boned
Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled and cut into 1.5 cm thick chips

content.text: Method
Soak and dry the chips

Cut the {{potato}} of peeled potatoes into 1.5 cm chips. Sit them in a bowl of cold water for 30 minutes; the soak draws out surface starch and the chips fry crisper. Drain. Spread on a clean tea towel and pat dry — every drop matters for safety in hot oil.
Blanch the chips at 140°C

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil in a wide, heavy pan to 140°C. Lower the chips in batches into the oil; don't crowd. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes — the chips soften through but stay pale. Lift onto kitchen paper.
Blanching
in two stages is the chip-shop secret.
Heat the oil to frying temperature
Bring the oil up to 190°C. This is the temperature for the second chip fry and for the fish. A thermometer matters; eyeballing oil temperature is unreliable.
Mix the batter at the last moment

Sift {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{cornflour}} of cornflour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt into a cold bowl. Pour in {{beer}} of ice-cold beer. Whisk just to combine; the batter should be lumpy and the colour of pale cream. Don't smooth it out — the lumps fry into bubbled crust.
Coat and fry the fish

Pat the {{cod-fillet}} of cod fillets dry. Dust with the extra {{plain-flour}} of flour set aside for dusting; tap off the excess. Hold a fillet by the tail end, dip into the batter, lift, and let the excess run off for two seconds. Lower the fillet gently into the 190°C oil, away from you. Fry for 6 to 8 minutes, turning once, until the batter is a deep amber and crisp. The fish inside flakes when pressed gently with a fork. Lift onto kitchen paper.
Crisp the chips at 190°C

While the fish drains, return the blanched chips to the 190°C oil in batches. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes until the shells are gold and shatter-crisp. Lift, drain on kitchen paper, scatter with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes.
Serve
Plate two fillets and a generous pile of chips per portion. Malt vinegar at the table; lemon wedges; mushy peas if you have them. Eat immediately; the batter softens within five minutes off the heat.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Pat the fillets dry. Dust with flour. The batter clings to the flour, not the fish.

items.cause: Batter overmixed or rested too long; flat beer.
items.fix: Mix at the last moment, lumps and all. Use beer straight from the fridge.

items.cause: Single fry, or oil too cold for the second pass.
items.fix: Blanch at 140°C; rest; crisp at 190°C. The two-stage fry is non-negotiable.

items.symptom: Oil temperature drops when food goes in
items.cause: Too much food in the pan at once.
items.fix: Fry in small batches. The thermometer should never drop more than 15°C.

items.symptom: Fish raw inside, batter dark outside
items.cause: Oil too hot, or fillet too thick.
items.fix: 190°C is the ceiling. For thick loins, drop to 180°C and fry 8 to 9 minutes.

content.text: Variations

Haddock is the northern English chip-shop fish; pollock and hake work just as well and cost less. Sparkling water in place of beer gives an alcohol-free batter. A tablespoon of vinegar in the chip-soaking water makes the chips even crisper. For mushy peas: simmer dried marrowfat peas with a pinch of bicarb until they collapse.
Where this dish lives

Fish and chips is the national dish of England in the way that other countries claim a moussaka or a paella. Born in the back streets of Victorian London and Lancashire, it fed mill towns through two world wars and remains the Friday-night standard in coastal towns from Whitby to Hastings. The cook who masters the home version has done something quietly impressive: replicated, on a domestic hob, what a chip-shop fryer does at industrial scale.
